author: @SaltGrains_Fready
text:
Perfectly Brilliant !! 
You have just opened a huge can of worms (or belts in this matter) as the old expression goes.
I have a perfect functioning old pedal exercise bike with a 20 inch wheel, in a small portable frame (about 3-4 KG) from decades ago restored from sitting by a scrap yard pile that I want to do this with. It's just a wheel that's chain driven by pedals which is ideal for a project like this.
The inside out belt can attach over the solid rubber Tyre and either turn the flywheel on the generator with the belt affixed onto it as you show, or have another of these belts connect it to belt drive the generator on an external frame. You have no idea how many times I thought up ways to connect it with friction drive from the wheel to a generator, or carving out the Tyre with a V belt groove and so on. THIS makes it into a DIY piece of cake.

author: @andywestphall2599

author: @Martin-c5m3k
text: I know nothing about these things, but would it be possible to make one of these gears, then scan it and then 3D print, which could then be scaled to any size required?

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
There are Usually fairly good engineering drawings of the tooth profiles available. If one were to try creating a 3D model  of such that would likely be way to go. Nb a good many toothed belts have  semi circular as on driven/driving pullies all that matters are that they have groves that the belt tooth fit into.

While it can sometimes be a bit finicity to get such to feed through printer, it is possible to 3D print flexible material. I probably wouldn't 3D print a timing belt as such material not the best under tension. Proper timing belts will have lengths of cord/wire running inside them, so they can resist tension.

author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
I'd watched you do this in an earlier video, and was just thinking about it yesterday.
I'm pondering a Wind Wall of Ugrinsky Columns along a 20' stretch of my fence line, and was thinking that I could use this to "chain" the outputs together, by gearing the bases. The Ugrinsky's would, of course, need to alternate rotation.
I doubt it will be more than a Paper Project for me, for quite some time.

author: @colrodrick8784
text:
Brilliant.  And you’re so right.  Every mechanic who does services throws these in the bin.  I have a mate who has all the tools and hoists so of course he does our cars too.  One is due for a timing belt change in about 10,000 kms so there’s another one available (in Oz).

Just brilliant simplicity.  No wonder you’re excited.

author: @mark.guitar
text: I'm making a telescope mount at the moment and have been scratching my head about sourcing gearing. Enter RMS in as timely a manner as you like! Cheers.

author: @chrissscottt
text: It surprised me how good the condition of the timing belt in my old lexus was after 160,000 kms. They're amazingly hard wearing.

replies.author: @colrodrick8784
replies.text: Mostly they are.  Have a friend who had no idea about cars; asked me what that rattling was.  Of course the timing belt was way overdue for replacement.  I think about 40,000 km from memory.  No damage caused but it shows that they last a lot longer than the recommended changes.

author: @zapaholic
text: It seems to me you would need to get the circumference of each part to very tight tolerances otherwise they're not going to mesh well?

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text: Not THAT precise. It is an elastic-type material with a lot of bounce, and over time, will most likely wear together long before it wears out.

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: How much wear and how quickly? Meshed gears require lubricant. The general wisdom is that oil perishes rubber compounds or maybe just some rubber compounds. Then what is rubber anyway these days.  ..and why did the rubber compound of my Nike shoes simply self-destruct with hardly any usage ?

author: @claudesully
text: thumb up... mate🥸🥸🥸

author: @GrandadTinkerer
text:
Great idea Rob.
But wouldn't the circumference of the wheel have to be an exact multiple of the tooth pitch?

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: Exactly... you can't just cut these to size. However, if you employ a tension mechanism, then it can be used provided you have good friction between the belt and the driving mechanism etc

replies.author: @GrandadTinkerer
replies.text: Which would ok if Rob was using this as two gears with a belt in between. However, the inended use, as far as I understand, is two gears directly in mesh with each other.@marcfruchtman9473

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
It's actually easier than you think, with a bit of math and good cutting skills.
And don't forget to take in the thickness whatever adhesive you use, unless it's something like Superglue.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @TimeSurfer206 Well, that "bit of math"... is the key, because if you don't make sure that the circumference evenly fits the belt, then you will get 1 tooth that is split...

author: @stevestogsdill5791
text: You guys make the best content on YouTube!

author: @st33ldi9ital
text: all that lead up and talk of hating making gears and then the inverted belt... I thought you were going to go for just belt drive at first.. didn't expect the making a gear out of it lol.

author: @ChristieNel
text: So elegant.  The obvious problem of course is when the ends don't meet up right.  But nothing that can't be fixed with a layer of double sided tape to increase the radius, right?

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
It's actually easier than you think. Just cut the belt at the same point in relation to the cogs each time, as in, "At the end of each cog," for example.
This, naturally, dictates the length of the belt, which then dictates the Circumfence (Shoutout to Sir Terry Pratchett) of the wheel..

author: @grendel1960a
text: after a comment made by me a friend who designs drive systems for telescopes worldwide changed over from using worm gears to belt systems as they generally generate less backlash, and when controlled by stepper drives can be as precise as needed.

author: @adamfabing8250
text: the most simple gears I;ve ever seen was in an old cassette player...the gears were literally foam circles that just touched each other. It was dead quiet. Simple for projects, just turn down whatever diameter you need with no special tools. obviously if the application needs to transfer any kind of real power this wont work. But rubber stretched over a circle does, up to a point. good for fast prototype

replies.author: @adamfabing8250
replies.text: oh yeah another advantage is no lash, and no need to use a tensioner with other direct drive non toothed gears.

replies.author: @st33ldi9ital
replies.text: you'd be surprised at the amount of torque you can get just using rubber wheels or tires...

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
